trace.code:
def bsearch(a, t):
    lo, hi = 0, len(a) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if a[mid] == t: return mid
        if a[mid] < t: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1

trace.code:
def climb(n):
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = 1
    dp[1] = 1
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2]
    return dp[n]

We run `kth_largest([3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], 2)` and record the real execution. The row of 3 slots you see IS a binary tree in disguise — the children of slot i live at slots 2i+1 and 2i+2, and the rule of the heap is simply: every parent stays ≤ its children. Watch slot 0: whatever sifts its way there is always the minimum.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 3 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 0. It sifted all the way to slot 0 — a new minimum.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 2 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 0 (2 slots shifted on its way). It sifted all the way to slot 0 — a new minimum.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 1 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 0 (2 slots shifted on its way). It sifted all the way to slot 0 — a new minimum.

Line 7 runs `heapq.heappop(heap)`. The root 1 is REMOVED — the root is always the minimum, that is the heap's whole promise. The last element 2 takes slot 0 and sifts DOWN, settling at slot 0; the new root is 2.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 5 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 2. Its parent, slot 0 holding 2, is ≤ it — so the sift stops here and the heap property holds.

Line 7 runs `heapq.heappop(heap)`. The root 2 is REMOVED — the root is always the minimum, that is the heap's whole promise. The last element 5 takes slot 0 and sifts DOWN, settling at slot 1; the new root is 3.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 6 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 2. Its parent, slot 0 holding 3, is ≤ it — so the sift stops here and the heap property holds.

Line 7 runs `heapq.heappop(heap)`. The root 3 is REMOVED — the root is always the minimum, that is the heap's whole promise. The last element 6 takes slot 0 and sifts DOWN, settling at slot 1; the new root is 5.
Line 5 runs `heapq.heappush(heap, x)`. 4 enters at the end and SIFTS UP, settling at slot 0 (2 slots shifted on its way). It sifted all the way to slot 0 — a new minimum.

Line 7 runs `heapq.heappop(heap)`. The root 4 is REMOVED — the root is always the minimum, that is the heap's whole promise. The last element 5 takes slot 0 and sifts DOWN, settling at slot 0; the new root is 5.

Execution finishes and the call returns 5. Read the final row as a tree one last time — slot 0 over slots 1 and 2, and so on down — and notice the promise held at every step: the parent never exceeded its children, so the minimum was always one glance away.

trace.code:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.next = None

def build(vals):
    head = None
    for v in reversed(vals):
        node = ListNode(v)
        node.next = head
        head = node
    return head

def reverse(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev

lst = build([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])

steps.explanation: We run reverse(lst) and watch the chain itself: every box is a real node object from the run, every arrow is its live next-reference. Boxes never move — only the arrows and the named pointers do, and that is exactly where your eyes should be.
head starts on the node holding 1 — remember which box it stands on; every decision below is made from where the named pointers stand.
prev is now None — it has walked off the end of the chain. Reading prev.next here would crash: this exact moment is where null-checks earn their keep.
curr starts on the node holding 1 — remember which box it stands on; every decision below is made from where the named pointers stand.
nxt starts on the node holding 2 — remember which box it stands on; every decision below is made from where the named pointers stand.
The arrow out of the node holding 1 is CUT: it pointed at the node holding 2, and now it points at None — this node just became the tail of its chain.
prev advances to the node holding 1. The pointer moved — the nodes did not; walking a list never touches the boxes, only where we are looking.
curr advances to the node holding 2. The pointer moved — the nodes did not; walking a list never touches the boxes, only where we are looking.
nxt advances to the node holding 3. The pointer moved — the nodes did not; walking a list never touches the boxes, only where we are looking.

We sort the 4 intervals by start: [1,3] [2,6] [8,10] [15,18]. Sorting is the whole trick — once sorted, any interval that overlaps something can only overlap the island RIGHT BEFORE it, so one left-to-right pass decides everything.
[1,3] opens the first island — nothing exists yet to overlap with, so it goes straight into the answer.
Does [2,6] overlap the current island [1,3]? Its start 2 is ≤ the island's end 3 — YES, they touch. The island absorbs it and now reaches [1,6] (end = max(3, 6) = 6).
Does [8,10] overlap the current island [1,6]? Its start 8 is > the island's end 6 — NO, there is a gap. The island is sealed and [8,10] opens a NEW one.
Does [15,18] overlap the current island [8,10]? Its start 15 is > the island's end 10 — NO, there is a gap. The island is sealed and [15,18] opens a NEW one.
Every interval has been consumed: 3 islands remain — [1,6] [8,10] [15,18]. That is the answer the real run returned: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

trace.code:
def msort(a, lo, hi):
    if hi - lo <= 1: return
    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
    msort(a, lo, mid)
    msort(a, mid, hi)
    merged = []
    i, j = lo, mid
    while i < mid and j < hi:
        if a[i] <= a[j]: merged.append(a[i]); i += 1
        else: merged.append(a[j]); j += 1
    merged.extend(a[i:mid]); merged.extend(a[j:hi])
    a[lo:hi] = merged

arr = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]

steps.explanation: We run msort(arr, 0, 7) and watch two pictures at once: the array with the ACTIVE BAND lit (everything dimmed is another call's job), and the recursion tree growing as the problem splits. Divide and conquer means never thinking about more than one band at a time.
We enter msort(0..6): the problem is now JUST these 7 cells — watch the focus band shrink onto them. Nothing outside the band can be touched by this call; that discipline is the whole design.
New state appears: lo = 0, hi = 7 — keep your eye on these values; every pointer move below is decided by them.
`mid` starts at index 3, where the value is 3. These positions are from the real run — the arrows you see are exactly where the pointers stood at this moment.

We enter msort(0..2): msort(0..6) hands down the band 0..2 and the problem is now JUST these 3 cells — watch the focus band shrink onto them. Nothing outside the band can be touched by this call; that discipline is the whole design.
The bookkeeping updates: hi becomes 3. This is the decision the comparison just produced — the pointers only move BECAUSE values like these said so.
`mid` moves to index 1, where the value is 27. These positions are from the real run — the arrows you see are exactly where the pointers stood at this moment.
msort(0..0) is the BASE CASE: a band of one cell is already sorted by definition — the splitting stops here, and this tiny certainty is what every bigger answer will be built from.

We run count_components(5, [[0, 1], [1, 2], [3, 4]]) on this exact graph and watch the algorithm's own variables — the node being processed, the distance table, the frontier. Every value below was recorded from the real run; the picture can never disagree with the code.

0 starts as its own root — every element begins as a lone set of one, and the parent table simply points each element at itself until a union merges it into a family. 1 starts as its own root — every element begins as a lone set of one, and the parent table simply points each element at itself until a union merges it into a family. 2 starts as its own root — every element begins as a lone set of one, and the parent table simply points each element at itself until a union merges it into a family.
Now 0 is taken up next and becomes the node under the pointer. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along 0's edges.
Now 1 is taken up next and becomes the node under the pointer. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along 1's edges.
Union: 0's root becomes 1 — the two sets merge into one family, and any future find() on 0's side will now walk up to 1. Watch the forest get flatter as unions accumulate.
Now 2 is taken up next and becomes the node under the pointer. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along 2's edges.
Union: 1's root becomes 2 — the two sets merge into one family, and any future find() on 1's side will now walk up to 2. Watch the forest get flatter as unions accumulate.
Now 3 is taken up next and becomes the node under the pointer. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along 3's edges.
Now 4 is taken up next and becomes the node under the pointer. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along 4's edges.
Union: 3's root becomes 4 — the two sets merge into one family, and any future find() on 3's side will now walk up to 4. Watch the forest get flatter as unions accumulate.
The walk is complete and the call returns 2. Every number in the final table was earned by a relaxation you watched happen — that is the whole proof, performed in front of you.

We run dijkstra(g, 'A') on this exact graph and watch the algorithm's own variables — the node being processed, the distance table, the frontier. Every value below was recorded from the real run; the picture can never disagree with the code.

The table starts with A = "inf" — the starting point costs nothing to reach, and every other node begins unknown. The table starts with B = "inf" — the starting point costs nothing to reach, and every other node begins unknown. The table starts with C = "inf" — the starting point costs nothing to reach, and every other node begins unknown. …and 1 more table updates land in this same moment — the table panel shows them all.
Relaxation: through the current node, A is reachable in 0 — better than the "inf" we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 0:A — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.

Now A is taken out of the frontier and becomes the node under the pointer. Its tentative distance is 0 — the smallest of everything still waiting, which is exactly why it goes next. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along A's edges.
Relaxation: through A, B is reachable in 4 — better than the "inf" we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 4:B — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.
Relaxation: through A, C is reachable in 1 — better than the "inf" we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 1:C, 4:B — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.

Now C is taken out of the frontier and becomes the node under the pointer. Its tentative distance is 1 — the smallest of everything still waiting, which is exactly why it goes next. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along C's edges.
Relaxation: through C, B is reachable in 3 — better than the 4 we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 3:B, 4:B — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.
Relaxation: through C, D is reachable in 6 — better than the "inf" we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 3:B, 4:B, 6:D — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.

Now B is taken out of the frontier and becomes the node under the pointer. Its tentative distance is 3 — the smallest of everything still waiting, which is exactly why it goes next. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along B's edges.
Relaxation: through B, D is reachable in 4 — better than the 6 we knew before, so the table is UPDATED. This one comparison, repeated everywhere, is the entire algorithm.
The queue now holds 4:B, 4:D, 6:D — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.
The queue now holds 4:D, 6:D — these are the candidates still waiting for their turn; everything the algorithm will do next comes out of this list.

Now D is taken out of the frontier and becomes the node under the pointer. Its tentative distance is 4 — the smallest of everything still waiting, which is exactly why it goes next. Whatever we learn in this moment, we learn by looking along D's edges.
The queue is now empty — nothing is waiting anymore, which is exactly the signal that the algorithm is about to finish.

The walk is complete and the call returns {"A":0,"B":3,"C":1,"D":4}. Every number in the final table was earned by a relaxation you watched happen — that is the whole proof, performed in front of you.
